narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 72.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows you ranked in the 2nd decile for Year 1 grades and 3rd decile for Year 3 grades at your school—both well below the typical 6th decile averages—along with an undergraduate GPA of 2.8 (versus the usual 3.21) and an LSAT score of 35 (under the average 36.7). You come from a high family income bracket, which is stronger than most, and you're a white male planning full-time studies, aligning with the majority of applicants in those demographics. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure more than any other factor. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also nudging the outlook negatively compared to stronger performers. Your high family income came third, surprisingly contributing toward the failure prediction despite being an advantage over averages. Following that were your LSAT score and racial background, then your undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status, each playing smaller but still unfavorable roles in the overall assessment. In essence, your academic deciles stood out as the top concerns, far below what the model sees in students who typically pass the bar. Compared to peers who succeed, your early and later school rankings lag significantly, while your other metrics are closer to average but didn't offset them enough. To boost your future bar passage odds, focus on what you can realistically improve: retake the LSAT for a higher score, build stronger study habits proven to lift bar performance, or pursue targeted academic prep that mirrors top decile students. With dedication, addressing these academic signals could shift your profile toward the passing patterns the model favors. You're capable of turning this around—many students do by prioritizing rigorous practice and skill-building. Keep pushing forward.
